title-group.article-title: Genomic insights into the broad-spectrum antifungal activity of a novel Paenibacillus polymyxa strain X-11

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The Paenibacillus polymyxa group is potentially useful for regulating plant growth and controlling plant diseases. We previously isolated a novel P. polymyxa strain (X-11) that can promote plant growth. However, its antagonistic effects on plant pathogenic fungi and the underlying mechanisms therein remain unclear. To promote its utility for controlling plant diseases, strain X-11 was characterized in terms of its antagonistic effects on plant pathogenic fungi, genome sequence, genes related to antimicrobial activities, and extracellular antifungal proteins.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Strain X-11 had significant antagonistic effects on several plant pathogenic fungi: Fusarium fujikuroi, Fusarium oxysporum, Magnaporthe oryzae, Botrytis cinerea, and Exserohilum turcicum. Its genome was revealed to contain 5,014 protein-coding genes as well as 49 tRNA and 10 rRNA sequences. The genome was most similar to that of P. polymyxa SC2, with 68.80% of its unique genes homologous to sequences in strain SC2. Furthermore, 85 genes related to antimicrobial activities were identified in the strain X-11 genome, including glycosyl hydrolase genes (20), serine protease genes (8), metalloproteinase genes (23), lanthionine antibiotic biosynthesis-related genes (6), and non-lanthionine antibiotic biosynthesis-related genes (2). Additionally, 14 nonribosomal peptide synthetase genes and 12 polyketide synthase genes were detected. Proteins with antifungal activities were identified in the inhibition zone between strain X-11 and Fusarium proliferatum (rice pathogen), including serine protease, lysophospholipase, metalloproteinase, glycoside hydrolase, β-glucosidase, peptidase M24, and amidase. Notably, bacillopeptidase F and bacillolysin were the most abundant antifungal proteins, suggesting that they may be the main contributors to strain X-11’s antifungal effects. An adenylation domain-containing protein involved in non-ribosomal peptide synthesis was also identified.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The genomic characterization of strain X-11, coupled with experimental confirmation of its broad-spectrum antifungal activity, provides a strong genetic and functional basis for its development as a promising biocontrol agent against plant fungal diseases.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Paenibacillus polymyxa belongs to the Firmicutes and the Paenibacillaceae [1, 2]. Many P. polymyxa strains have important uses in agriculture [3], and their antimicrobial properties and regulatory effects on plant growth have been extensively studied [4–6]. From a metabolic perspective, P. polymyxa promotes plant growth by fixing nitrogen [7, 8], solubilizing phosphorus [9], producing siderophores as well as plant growth regulators [10, 11], and inducing systemic resistance [12, 13]. Furthermore, certain strains have antifungal properties relevant for controlling plant diseases [14–16]. For example, strain JY1-5 may be useful for preventing gray mold in tomato [17], while strain Sx3 may protect rice from white leaf blight [18]. Strain WLY78 reportedly enhances cucumber root and bud growth, while also increasing dry weight, via nitrogen fixation [19]. Earlier studies showed that P. polymyxa can effectively control Cercospora-induced leaf spot disease [20] and enhance cucumber defense responses to Fusarium oxysporum [21].

A recent study demonstrated that P. polymyxa effectively protects Brassica crops from clubroot disease caused by Plasmodiophora brassicae, which is a notoriously challenging pathogen to control [22]. In a recent study, P. polymyxa D41 was observed to synthesize zinc nanoparticles mediating wheat resistance to Fusarium culmorum [23]. Notably, P. polymyxa strains can enhance the growth of various crops, while also protecting against diseases, making them valuable biocontrol candidates [24]. As of October 2025, the NCBI database contains 335 biological samples (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/biosample/?term=Paenibacillus%20polymyxa) and 142 genomes (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/all/?term=Paenibacillus%20polymyxa) of P. polymyxa, reflecting the substantial research on this bacterium. However, despite its biocontrol potential and extensive genomic data, mechanistic studies on P. polymyxa have primarily focused on identifying functional genes and antimicrobial substances [25–27], with few integrated genomic–metabolomic analyses elucidating biocontrol mechanisms at the molecular level.

In our previous study, we isolated a bacterium with plant growth-promoting effects in the rhizosphere of wheat, and identified it as a novel strain of P. polymyxa X-11, it has the functions of fixing nitrogen, reducing nitrate, and producing indole, as well as increasing peroxidase activity in tomato leaves and superoxide dismutase activity in rice leaves, demonstrating strong application potential [28]. However, most of the studies on P. polymyxa have examined its plant growth-promoting properties, while also exploring its efficacy for controlling plant diseases [29, 30]. The growth-promoting effects of plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria may be associated with antimicrobial activities [5]. The objective of the current study was to analyze the antifungal mechanism of strain X-11 from genomic and metabolic perspectives, thereby providing insights into its potential use for managing plant diseases. To achieve this, we determined the inhibitory effects of strain X-11 on plant pathogenic fungi. We then sequenced and analyzed its genome, after which we identified the genes encoding extracellular antimicrobial proteins.

sec.title: Preparation of media for culturing P. polymyxa X-11 and plant pathogenic fungi
sec.p: Potato sucrose broth (PSB) was prepared by dissolving extract (200 g potato boiled in water for 10 min) and 20.0 g sucrose in 1,000 mL distilled water, with the resulting medium retaining its natural pH (6.0–7.0). Potato sucrose agar (PSA) was prepared by adding 20 g agar powder to the PSB mixture. Both media were sterilized by autoclaving at 121 °C for 20 min. Both PSB and PSA have been confirmed as suitable culture media for the growth of strain X-11 [28].

A composite medium was prepared following a published method that was modified appropriately [31]. Specifically, the medium consisted of 1.2 g sucrose, 2.6 g corn starch, 1.2 g peptone, 0.24 g MgSO4·7H2O, 0.6 g CaCO3, 1.2 g K2HPO4, 6.0 g soybean meal, 6.0 g corn meal, and 1,000 mL distilled water. After adjusting the pH to 7.0–7.2, the medium was sterilized by autoclaving at 121 °C for 30 min.

sec.title: Preparation of P. polymyxa X-11 bacterial suspension
sec.p: P. polymyxa strain X-11 was maintained in our laboratory and deposited in the China General Microbiological Culture Collection Center (CGMCC No. 20476). Primary cultivation was performed using PSA plates. A bacterial colony was aseptically transferred to the composite medium, which was subsequently incubated at 30 °C with shaking (180 rpm) in darkness for 48 h to obtain a spore suspension (1 × 109 CFU/mL).

sec.title: Preparation of plant pathogenic fungi
sec.p: Our laboratory maintains a collection of fungal plant pathogens, including the following species isolated from infected plants: Fusarium fujikuroi (causes rice bakanae disease) [32], F. oxysporum (causes rice seedling blight) [33], Magnaporthe oryzae (causes rice blast) [34], Botrytis cinerea (causes tomato gray mold) [35], Exserohilum turcicum (Pass) Leonard and Suggs (causes corn northern leaf blight) [36], and Fusarium proliferatum (causes rice spikelet rot disease) [37]. These fungal isolates were activated and cultured separately on PSA plates at 28 °C for 4 days in darkness before being used as plant pathogenic fungi.

sec.title: Determination of the antagonistic effects of P. polymyxa X-11 on plant pathogens
sec.p: The antagonistic effects of strain X-11 on plant pathogenic fungi were evaluated using a confrontation culture method involving PSA plates described in our published article [38], with three replicates per treatment. For each pathogenic fungus, an agar piece containing mycelium (activated and cultured on PSA plates) was placed at the center of a new PSA plate (90 mm diameter), which was then incubated for 48 h to promote growth. Strain X-11 cells were aseptically obtained from a colony that was cultivated on a PSA plate and immediately added to PSA plates that had been inoculated with a pathogenic fungus 48 h earlier. Specifically, strain X-11 cells were placed 22.5 mm from the pathogenic fungus (Fig. 1). PSA plates inoculated with only one pathogenic fungus (i.e., without strain X-11) were used as controls. All inoculated PSA plates were incubated at 28 °C for 4 days before assessing the antifungal effects of strain X-11 according to the fungal growth inhibition zone width and colony radius. For each plant pathogenic fungus, the growth inhibitory efficiency (%) was calculated using the following formula: (control colony radius − treatment colony radius)/control colony radius × 100.

caption.p: Antagonistic effects of P. polymyxa X-11 on plant pathogens. For A-E the upper row presents the growth of pathogenic fungi in the absence of strain X-11 (i.e., controls), whereas the lower row presents the inhibitory effects of strain X-11 (indicated by an arrow) on these pathogens. The scale bar in E is applicable to all images. In F: different letters on the columns denote statistically significant differences (p < 0.05)

sec.p: Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS 19.0 (IBM, Chicago, IL, USA). A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by the least significant difference (LSD) post hoc test was applied to evaluate (1) the width of the inhibition zone and colony radius, with significance set at p < 0.05, and (2) the inhibitory efficiency of strain X-11 against different plant pathogens, with significance set at p < 0.01.

sec.title: Sequencing of the P. polymyxa X-11 genome and analysis of antimicrobial protein-encoding genes
sec.p: Strain X-11 was cultured in LB medium for 24 h. Bacterial cells were collected via high-speed centrifugation (12,000 × g at room temperature for 10 min). Genomic DNA was extracted from pelleted bacterial cells using a QIAamp kit (Qiagen, Duesseldorf, Germany). DNA quality was determined using a NanoDrop 2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). High-quality DNA (A280/A260 = 1.8-2.0) was retained for genome sequencing, splicing, and assembly [39]; general gene functional annotation; and antimicrobial gene analysis [40]. Genome sequencing was performed on an Illumina HiSeq platform (e.g., HiSeq 2500/4000/X Ten) with paired-end reads (e.g., 2 × 150 bp), generating 6,913,142 of paired-end reads. The raw sequencing data have been deposited in the NCBI SRA database under accession number SUB15161604. In addition, strain X-11 genes were further annotated using the KAAS (an automatic genome annotation and pathway reconstruction server) (http://www.genome.jp/kegg/kaas/) [41], the functional annotation process, along with its key steps, is summarized as follows: (1) Repetitive sequences were identified in the strain X-11 genome by RepeatMasker (version4.1.0). (2) CRISPR repeats were predicted using CRT software 9 (version 1.2). (3) Strain X-11 protein sequences were used for a BLAST search in the Virulence Factors (VFs) database (http://www.mgc.ac.cn/VFs/) comprising bacterial VFs, and the search results were combined with the general gene functional annotation information to identify genes encoding VFs. The resulting annotations were then divided into Set A (core database) and Set B (full database). (4) Protein sequences were also used for a BLAST search in the Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (https://card.mcmaster.ca/), after which the search results and the general gene functional annotation information were combined to identify genes associated with drug resistance. (5) HMMER3 software (3.1b1) was used to compare integrin gene sequences with sequences in the Carbohydrate Active enZYmes database, which is divided into the following five main categories: glycoside hydrolases (GHs), glycosyltransferases (GTs), polysaccharide lyases (PLs), carbohydrate esterases (CEs), and oxidoreductase (auxiliary activities). Carbohydrate-binding modules (CBMs) were also included. Carbohydrate active enzyme annotation information corresponding to CBMs was obtained after the comparison. E-value < 1e-5 was set as the threshold for filtering. (6) LipoP software (version 1.0) was used to predict lipoproteins.

sec.title: Identification and analysis of P. polymyxa X-11 extracellular antimicrobial proteins
sec.p: To determine whether the strainX-11 can inhibit the growth of pathogenic fungi by secreting antibacterial active substances, we analyzed its extracellular proteins.

sec.title: Confrontation culture involving strain X-11 and F. proliferatum as well as sample collection
sec.p: F. proliferatum was activated on PSA medium, after which an agar piece containing mycelium was placed on the center of a new Petri dish containing PSA medium. After incubating in darkness at 28 °C for 2 days, bacterial cells of strain X-11 that were cultured on PSA medium in another Petri dish for 2 days were carefully picked up using an inoculation ring and then placed on the PSA medium previously inoculated with F. proliferatum (30 mm from the F. proliferatum inoculation site). After a 4-day cultivation, F. proliferatum growth was restricted by strain X-11, with an antifungal zone forming between the two microbes. Agar pieces were excised from the growth inhibition zone and placed in sterile centrifuge tubes for the extraction of proteins secreted by strain X-11 into the PSA medium.

sec.title: Protein extraction
sec.p: Samples were lysed and proteins were extracted using SDT buffer (4% SDS, 100 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM Dithiothreitol, pH 7.6). The extracted proteins were quantified using the BCA Protein Assay Kit (Bio-Rad Medical Products (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). All protein samples were stored at -80 °C. To analyze proteins, 20 µg of protein per sample was mixed with 5× loading buffer and boiled for 5 min. Proteins were then separated by 12.5% sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) (14 mA constant current for 90 min). Protein bands were visualized using a Coomassie Blue R-250 staining method, while the proteins were digested using trypsin. The resulting peptides were desalted using Empore™ SPE C18 Cartridges (standard density, 7 mm bed inner diameter, 3 mL volume; Sigma), concentrated via vacuum centrifugation, and reconstituted in 40 µL 0.1% (v/v) formic acid.

sec.title: Mass spectrometry analysis
sec.p: A liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) analysis (65 min) was performed using a Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) coupled with an Easy nLC instrument (Proxeon Biosystems, now Thermo Fisher Scientific). Peptides were loaded onto an Acclaim PepMap 100 reversed phase trap column (100 μm × 2 cm, nanoViper C18; Thermo Scientific), which was connected to an Easy Column C18 reversed phase analytical column (10 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 3 μm resin size; Thermo Scientific). The analysis was completed using buffer A (0.1% formic acid) and a linear gradient of buffer B (84% acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid) at a flow rate of 300 nL/min controlled by IntelliFlow technology.

The mass spectrometer was operated in the positive ion mode. In addition, MS data were acquired using a data-dependent top 10 method that selected the most abundant precursor ions from the survey scan range (m/z 300–1,800) for higher energy collision dissociation (HCD) fragmentation. The automatic gain control target was set to 3e6 and the maximum fill time allowed per spectrum was set to 10 ms. The dynamic exclusion duration was 40.0 s. Survey scans were acquired at a resolution of 70,000 at m/z 200, the resolution for HCD spectra was set to 17,500 at m/z 200, and the isolation width was 2 m/z. Normalized collision energy was 30 eV and the underfill ratio, which specifies the minimum percentage of the target value likely to be reached at the maximum fill time, was defined as 0.1%. The instrument was operated with the peptide recognition mode.

sec.title: Identification and quantification of peptides
sec.p: The MS raw data for each sample were combined and analyzed using the MaxQuant software for identifying and quantifying peptides. The relative levels of proteins were assessed using two complementary metrics: the number of confidently identified peptides and the MS signal intensity of those peptides. The related parameters were as follows: (1) Enzyme: trypsin; (2) Maximum number of missed cleavages: 2; (3) Fixed modifications: carbamidomethyl (C); (4) Variable modifications: oxidation (M), acetyl (protein N-terminal); (5) Database: public database (http://www.uniprot.org/), organism species “Paenibacillus polymyxa”; (6) Peptide FDR ≤ 0.01; (7) Protein FDR ≤ 0.01.

sec.title: Bioinformatic analyses
sec.p: Domain annotation: Protein sequences were used as queries to search the Pfam database hosted by InterPro using the InterProScan software to identify protein domain signatures.

Gene functional annotation was performed by aligning the predicted protein sequences of strain X-11 against four major databases: the Non-Redundant (NR) protein database (http://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/), the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) (http://www.kegg.jp), the Swiss-Prot manually annotated protein sequence database (http://www.uniprot.org/), and the Clusters of Orthologous Genes (COG) database (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/COG/). Sequence comparisons were conducted using BLASTP with an E-value cutoff of 1e-5 and a minimum identity threshold of 30%. The best hit for each query sequence was retained for functional assignment. Specifically, KEGG Orthology (KO) identifiers were assigned for pathway analysis.
A Venn diagram was generated using the R package VennDiagram (version 1.7.3) to illustrate the distribution of strain X-11 genes annotated against the NR, KEGG, Swissprot, and COG databases.

sec.title: Antagonistic effects of P. polymyxa X-11 on plant pathogens
sec.p: Strain X-11 had significant inhibitory effects on plant pathogenic fungi, even though it was added to PSA plates 48 h after plant pathogenic fungi were added. Specifically, the average growth inhibition zone widths for F. fujikuroi, F. oxysporum, M. oryzae, and B. cinerea were 6.37, 5.33, 12.67, and 18.83 mm, respectively, with growth inhibition efficiencies of 41.76%, 69.14%, 57.48%, and 70.89% respectively (Fig. 1A–D; Table 1). When the PSA medium was inoculated with strain X-11 and E. turcicum at the same time, the growth inhibition zone width was 15.76 mm. Moreover, E. turcicum hyphae were limited to approximately half of the PSA medium (i.e., the side lacking strain X-11), thus, the growth inhibitory efficiency was 100% (Fig. 1E; Table 1).

caption.p: Colony radius of control and P. polymyxa X-11 treated plant pathogens and its growth inhibitory efficiency

table-wrap-foot.p: “*” indicates the difference between the control (pathogen inoculated alone) and X-11 treatment (Pathogen under competitive cultivation of Strain X-11) was significantly (p < 0.01). Different letters within the same column denote statistically significant differences (p < 0.05)

sec.title: P. polymyxa X-11 genomic features
sec.p: The strain X-11 draft genome comprised 5,510,531 bp (213 contigs), of which 4,686,172 bp (i.e., 85.04%) were for genes encoding 5,014 proteins. The GC content of the genome was 46%. Moreover, sequences corresponding to 10 rRNAs and 49 tRNAs were detected in the genome. A total of 297 repeat regions spanning 146,955 bp (2.67% of the genome) were predicted in the strain X-11 genome. A CRISPR analysis revealed 27 repeats with an average length of 22 bp and an average spacer length of 54 bp. A total of 299 VFs (95 different types) were included in Set A, whereas 322 VFs (123 different types) belonged to Set B. Additionally, 204 drug resistance-related genes in 61 categories were identified. A carbohydrate active enzyme (CAZyme) analysis detected 145 GHs, 70 GTs, 56 CBMs, 53 CEs, 14 PLs, and 11 auxiliary activities. The cytoplasmic proteome of strain X-11 consisted of 3,657 proteins, 739 N-terminal transmembrane helices, and 348 signal peptides I, and 140 signal peptides II. According to the predicted subcellular localization, 2,526 proteins located in the cytoplasm (51.72% of all proteins), 885 proteins located in the inner membrane, 141 proteins located in the outer membrane, 80 proteins were secreted, 126 proteins located in the periplasm, and 1,126 proteins were associated with the cell wall or their localization was unknown (Table 2).

caption.p: P. polymyxa X-11 genomic features and annotated genes

sec.title: P. polymyxa X-11 genome annotation results
Number of strain X-11 genes annotated using each database
sec.p: Among the 4,884 unique protein coding genes in the strain X-11 genome, 4,830 (98.89%), 2,100 (43.00%), 3,641 (74.55%), and 3,497 (71.60%) were annotated using the NR, KEGG, Swiss-Prot, and COG databases, respectively. The TrEMBL database was used to annotate 4,793 genes (98.14% of the unique genes). A total of 1,971 genes (40.36%) were annotated using all databases. Figure 2 presents the number of genes annotated using various databases.

caption.p: Venn diagram of the number of P. polymyxa X-11 genes annotated in various databases

sec.title: Strain X-11 genes annotated according to species in the TrEMBL database
sec.p: Of the 4,793 genes of strain X-11 annotated using the TrEMBL database, 4,431 were associated with P. polymyxa species, accounting for 90.72% of the unique genes in strain X-11 and 92.45% of the genes annotated using this database. Within this subset, 3,360 genes were associated with P. polymyxa SC2 (68.80% of the unique genes). Strain X-11 was associated with P. polymyxa strains, such as E681 and CR1, as well as other Paenibacillus strains (e.g., Paenibacillus sp. Aloe-11, P. terrae HPL-003, and P. peoriae) (Fig. 3).

caption.p: P. polymyxa X-11 genes annotated using the TrEMBL database

sec.title: Strain X-11 genes annotated with KEGG metabolic pathways
sec.p: According to the KEGG annotation results, the number of genes in each of the five major metabolic pathway categories was as follows: “Metabolism” (2,173 genes), “Environmental information processing” (597 genes), “Genetic information processing” (276 genes), “Cellular processes” (93 genes), and “Organismal systems” (25 genes) (Fig. 4).

caption.p: P. polymyxa X-11 genes associated with KEGG pathways

sec.title: P. polymyxa X-11 genes encoding ribosome-synthesized and non-ribosome-synthesized antimicrobial proteins and polyketosynthases
Genes encoding ribosome-synthesized antimicrobial proteins
sec.p: The strain X-11 genome included 59 genes encoding ribosome-synthesized antimicrobial proteins. Specifically, 20 genes encoded glycosyl hydrolases (e.g., endoglucanase, exoglucanase precursor, and chitinase). Of the 31 protease genes that were identified, eight encoded serine proteases or serine protease precursors (e.g., bacillopeptidase F precursor and subtilisin-like serine proteases). Twenty-three genes encoded metalloproteinases (e.g., bacillolysin precursors and aminopeptidases). Furthermore, six genes encoding lanthionine antibiotic biosynthetic proteins were identified; these proteins were associated with the production of paenibacillin, subtilin, nisin, and mersacidin/lichenicidin. Notably, two antilisterial bacteriocin subtilosin biosynthesis protein (AlbA)-encoding genes were detected in the strain X-11 genome (Table 3).

caption.p: Ribosomal antimicrobial proteins coding genes of P. polymyxa X-11

sec.title: Genes related to the synthesis of non-ribosomal peptides and polyketones
sec.p: The 26 genes in the strain X-11 genome encoding non-ribosomally synthesized lipopeptides and polyketosynthases included 14 genes encoding non-ribosomal peptide synthetases (NRPSs). The identified genes responsible for producing antibiotics encoded gramicidin S synthases, linear gramicidin synthases, plipastatin synthase, surfactin synthase, and bacilysin biosynthesis oxidoreductase. Our analysis also revealed the presence of 12 polyketosynthase genes in the strain X-11 genome (Table 4).

caption.p: Nonribosomal peptide synthetases and polyketide synthase genes in P. polymyxa X-11

sec.title: Extracellular antimicrobial proteins secreted by P. polymyxa X-11
sec.p: After a 4-day confrontation culture, the control F. proliferatum colony radius was 42.0 mm. At the interface between strain X-11 and F. proliferatum on the PSA medium, an arc-shaped growth inhibition zone was detected, with a minimum width of 9.0 mm, whereas the distance between the center and the F. proliferatum colony front was 15.5 mm. The antifungal efficiency was determined to be 63.0% (Fig. 5). Furthermore, proteins were extracted from the PSA medium collected from the growth inhibition zone (total protein concentration of 0.61 µg/µL). We identified 79 peptides of 43 proteins, including eight proteins with antimicrobial activities (serine protease, lysophospholipase, neutral metalloproteinase, glycoside hydrolase, β-glucosidase, peptidase M24, neutral metalloproteinase, and amidase) (Table 5).

caption.p: Confrontation culture of P. polymyxa X-11 and the rice ear rot pathogen (F. proliferatum)
Partial peptide fragments of antifungal proteins identified in the F. proliferatum growth Inhibition zone in PSA plates inoculated with P. polymyxa X-11

tr.td: Amino acid adenylation domain-containing protein (Adenylation family)

sec.p: Notably, more peptide fragments with high intensity were detected for both bacillopeptidase F (serine protease) and bacillolysin (neutral metalloproteinase) than for the other identified antimicrobial proteins (Table 6), indicating these two proteins were likely the main antifungal compounds. The closest amino acid sequence matches for these proteins in the NCBI NR database are presented in Table 5. The partial mass spectrograms in Fig. 6 were used to identify these proteins.

caption.p: Identified peptide fragments of bacillopeptidase F and Bacillolysin in the F. proliferatum growth Inhibition zone in PSA plates inoculated with P. polymyxa X-11

table-wrap-foot.p: Numbers 1 and 2 correspond to 21, 22; 3 correspond 29, respectively, in Table 2

caption.p: LC-MS/MS spectrogram of P. polymyxa X-11 extracellular antimicrobial proteins. A and B bacillopeptidase F, C bacillolysin

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The annotation of the strain X-11 genome revealed significant information regarding VFs, drug resistance-related genes, and CAZymes. Our analysis identified 299 VFs (classified into 95 distinct types) in the core database (Set A), while the comprehensive database (Set B) contained 322 VFs representing 123 different types. Additionally, we detected 204 drug resistance-related genes from 61 functional categories. The CAZyme profile, which was particularly noteworthy, comprised 145 GHs, 70 GTs, 56 CBMs, 53 CEs, 14 PLs, and 11 enzymes with auxiliary activities (Table 2). These proteins may play significant roles in the environmental adaptability and nutrient acquisition of the strain X-11. The identified VFs, which are likely involved in core biocontrol mechanisms, included well-characterized antimicrobial compounds (e.g., NRPSs and PKSs) that have inhibitory effects on phytopathogens (e.g., Fusarium solani and Macrophomina phaseolina) [49, 50]. Moreover, the VF EsxA reportedly induces plant disease resistance [33]. CAZymes are fundamental for microbial carbon acquisition. Variation in CAZyme gene repertoires drives divergence in carbon utilization strategies and niche specialization among microorganisms. This allows distinct microbial populations to occupy separate ecological niches within the rhizosphere, thereby reducing direct competition, enhancing community stability [51]. In strain X-11, CAZymes may play pivotal roles in rhizosphere colonization and nutrient acquisition. Of which, GHs degrade plant residues (e.g., glucan and xylan) to release carbon sources, while also generating oligosaccharide signaling molecules that activate induced systemic resistance [52, 53]. Additionally, chitinases directly lyse fungal cell walls to suppress infections [54, 55]. Evidently, the mechanism underlying the biocontrol effects of strain X-11 appears to involve two core components: (1) VFs (NRPS/PKS/EsxA) and CAZymes with direct antagonistic effects on pathogens and (2) diverse drug resistance-related genes and CAZymes that collectively enhance ecological fitness in complex rhizosphere environments.

The recent expansion of microbial genome data has greatly facilitated the discovery of novel antimicrobial peptides. In line with this trend, Paenibacillus species are known to produce diverse antimicrobial agents, including peptide antibiotics, glycosyl hydrolases, proteases, and compounds synthesized by NRPS and PKS [40, 56–62]. Building on this foundation, our integrated genomic and proteomic analysis of P. polymyxaX-11 provides a molecular rationale for its observed broad-spectrum antifungal activity. Genomic mining identified 85 genes linked to antimicrobial functions, encompassing genes for various hydrolases (e.g., glycoside hydrolases, serine proteases, metalloproteinases) and antibiotic biosynthesis (e.g., NRPS, PKS), highlighting its genetic potential to produce antifungal compounds. Critically, proteomic analysis of the inhibition zone between X-11 and F. proliferatum confirmed the secretion of multiple antifungal proteins, including serine proteases, metalloproteinases, and a lysophospholipase. Among these, bacillopeptidase F and bacillolysin were the most abundant, suggesting their role as key effectors. This finding directly links specific genomic potentials (e.g., the genes for these serine proteases) to observed antifungal effects. The high abundance of these proteases, known to degrade microbial membrane and cell wall proteins [47, 48], points to a primary mechanism where they compromise pathogen integrity. Furthermore, the detection of an adenylation-domain protein implies additional activity via non-ribosomal peptides. Therefore, the antagonism of strain X-11 is likely mediated by a synergistic combination of hydrolytic enzymes and antimicrobial peptides, a functional synergy between predicted gene clusters and validated secreted proteins that explains its broad-spectrum efficacy and underscores its biocontrol potential.

Comparable to strain X-11, P. polymyxa strain L1-9 exhibited inhibitory effects against Fusarium graminearum, Verticillium dahliae, F. oxysporum f. sp. vasinfectum, Alternaria alternata, Phytophthora capsici, B. cinerea Pers, Alternaria solani, and other plant pathogens [63]. A recent study showed P. polymyxa strain YLC1 has antagonistic effects on the kiwifruit bacterial canker pathogen (Pseudomonas syringae pv. actinidiae), whereas P. polymyxa strain P1 inhibits the germination of dormant P. brassicae Woron spores and effectively controls clubroot [64]. However, strain X-11 demonstrates exceptional antifungal efficacy for E. turcicum, achieving a remarkable 100% growth inhibitory efficiency, far surpassing the performance of currently known microbial agents. These findings suggest that exploiting P. polymyxa genes associated with antimicrobial activities may be a feasible approach for plant disease control. Furthermore, analyses of the genomic data of P. polymyxa strains have provided valuable insights into antimicrobial mechanisms [60]. In the present study, strain X-11 had antagonistic effects on all tested plant pathogens, reflecting its broad-spectrum antimicrobial potential. According to our genome sequence analysis, the strain X-11 genome harbors various types of antimicrobe-related genes, including genes encoding glycosyl hydrolases, serine proteases, lantibiotics, metalloproteinases, non-lantibiotic bacteriocin (rSAM/SkfB), NRPSs, and PKSs. The expression of genes encoding antimicrobial proteins resulted in the production of antimicrobial active substances during the inhibition of F. proliferatum growth by strain X-11. Its related strains such as P. polymyxa strain SC2, possesses coding genes for fusaricidin, polymyxin, endoglucanase, and xylanase, and has been demonstrated to induce disease resistance in pepper [5, 6]. In contrast, excepted nonribosomal peptide synthetase (NRPS) genes responsible for the synthesis of fusaricidin and polymyxin, P. polymyxastrain E681 harbors coding genes for plant growth hormones, antibiotics, hydrolytic enzymes [13, 65]. Therefore, although strain X-11 shares certain genes with these other strains, it exhibits distinct differences in its antimicrobial spectrum and the specific repertoire of antimicrobial genes.

Among the various antimicrobial mechanisms, the production of diverse lipopeptides with antimicrobial activities is considered one of the main mechanisms used by P. polymyxa strains. These lipopeptides include polymyxins [66], gatavalin, jolipeptin, tridecaptins, bacillomycin [67–69], polymyxin [70], gramicidin [71], fusaricidin [72], and paenilipoheptin [73]. Strain X-11 also harbors numerous gene clusters mediating the non-ribosomal synthesis of lipopeptides. Notably, gramicidin S is an extremely potent antimicrobial agent effective against a wide range of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria as well as several pathogenic fungi [74, 75]. Plipastatin, which is an antifungal compound, is one of the most important lipopeptides not produced by ribosomes. Plipastatin inhibits phospholipase A2 and biofilm formation and is highly toxic to fungi [76]. Genome analysis of strain X-11 revealed that the strain carries genes for the synthesis of surfactin, which is a cyclic non-ribosomal lipoheptapeptide. Surfactin is one of the most potent biosurfactants with antibacterial and antiviral activities, making it potentially useful for protecting plants from pathogen infections [77, 78]. It consists of an adenylation domain (A-domain) architecture (i.e., condensation-adenylation-PCP-thioesterase). Furthermore, genes encoding another A-domain non-ribosomal peptide were identified in the strain X-11 genome; this peptide reportedly has significant antimicrobial bioactivities [79]. A gene for the synthesis of bacilysin was also detected in the strain X-11 genome. Bacilysin, which is one of the simplest antimicrobial peptides synthesized by spore-producing bacteria [80, 81], can protect plants from crown gall disease [82], Phytophthora sojae (soybean pathogen) [83], and Xanthomonas oryzae (rice pathogen) [84].

Twelve PKS genes were identified in the strain X-11 genome (Table 3). The products of reactions catalyzed by PKSs are relevant to controlling plant diseases [85, 86]. Previous studies elucidated the association between NRPS genes and PKS genes, which form gene clusters in Paenibacillus strains [40, 65]. The expression of NRPS genes and the subsequent assembly of the products derived from NRPS-catalyzed reactions have also been analyzed [87, 88]. Fusaricidin-type antifungal compounds were obtained from lysed P. polymyxa cells using a methanol extraction procedure [29]. Another study used a matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization-time-of-flight mass spectrometry system to detect paenibacillin in the filtrate of P. polymyxa OSY-DF that was exposed to environmental stress conditions to induce paenibacillin-related gene expression [89]. The identified strain X-11 extracellular antimicrobial compounds included an amino acid adenylation domain-containing protein responsible for activating specific amino acids and loading them onto the carrier protein (PCP) of non-ribosomal peptides (NRPs) [90].

Different P. polymyxa strains have distinct antimicrobial mechanisms, indicating the presence of diverse functional genes [91]. This implies these strains may be a rich resource of antimicrobial genes. Hence, these strains and their genomes should be more comprehensively analyzed in terms of their utility for improving plant disease management practices. However, identifying and using these strains and gene resources for enhancing agricultural production may be challenging because the molecular mechanisms underlying their antimicrobial properties will need to be characterized before they can be applied for controlling plant diseases.

The genome sequence complements the genomic data available for Paenibacillus strains and provides the basis for future studies aimed at clarifying the molecular mechanisms underlying the inhibitory effects of these strains on plant pathogens. Strain X-11, which is a bacterium with antimicrobial properties, likely evolved multiple strategies that enable it to adapt to harsh environmental conditions and control plant diseases.

This study has several limitations that should be considered. First, while the antagonistic effects of strain X-11 against various plant pathogens were demonstrated in vitro, its practical efficacy in controlling diseases under real-world agricultural conditions remains unvalidated. Consequently, in planta trials are essential to translate these promising in vitro findings into proven biocontrol utility. Second, the functional validation of key genes identified in this study, particularly those encoding the detected extracellular antimicrobial proteins, was beyond the current scope. The antimicrobial roles of these candidate genes await confirmation through molecular techniques such as cloning, heterologous expression, and direct bioactivity assays. To address these limitations, our future work will focus on two parallel tracks: (i) conducting controlled greenhouse and field trials to evaluate the disease suppression efficacy of strain X-11 against major target pathogens in relevant crops, and (ii) employing genetic and biochemical approaches (e.g., targeted gene knockout, heterologous expression, and protein purification) to functionally validate the roles of key candidate genes and proteins identified in this study. These steps are crucial for advancing strain X-11 from a promising candidate to a validated biocontrol agent.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The genome of a novel strain (X-11) of P. polymyxa strain was analyzed, and the antagonistic effects of this strain on plant pathogenic fungi were confirmed. Notably, the strain X-11 genome contains 85 genes associated with antimicrobial activities. Furthermore, among the extracellular proteins of strain X-11 detected in the inhibition zone against the rice pathogen F. proliferatum, we identified several with antimicrobial activity. Notably, these included the high-abundance proteins bacillopeptidase F and bacillolysin. The findings of this study provide key insights into the molecular basis of strain X-11’s protective effects against plant pathogens, while also highlighting its promise as a biocontrol agent, warranting further investigation into its precise mechanisms and practical applications.
